message.content:
You are an adversarial refuter. ASSUME this finding is FALSE and try to prove it. Read the actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and find the code path / contract / test that makes the PoC impossible or the concern moot. Only conclude survives=true if you genuinely cannot disprove it.

Finding P-3: Pipeline builds the corpus unit-id set twice on the same corpus (redundant O(U) pass)
File: src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:445
Description: In `run_study`, line 435 builds `unit_ids = {u.unit_id for u in corpus.units}` inside the DISCOVERY branch to validate split ids, and line 445 builds an identical `corpus_unit_ids = {u.unit_id for u in corpus.units}` unconditionally for the feature-builder ghost-id check. When a study runs in DISCOVERY mode AND declares feature builders (the common case), the corpus units are iterated twice to construct the same set. Cheap to fix and trivially correct.
PoC: # A DISCOVERY-mode study with feature_builders triggers two full passes over corpus.units
# building the identical {unit_id} set (pipeline.py:435 and :445).

Return survives=false if you can refute it (with the specific reason), survives=true if it withstands a genuine disproof attempt.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

420	                "parsed source into canonical corpus",
421	                source_fingerprint=source_fp,
422	                corpus_fingerprint=cfp,
423	                n_units=len(corpus.units),
424	                n_relations=len(corpus.relations),
425	                source_version=source_version,
426	            )
427	            # SLO-relevant scale metrics to the tracker so corpus-size↔latency regressions are
428	            # analysable from the experiment store, not only the provenance trail (pass 3,
429	            # #F-029). Best-effort via track() (no-ops if no tracker/run_id).
430	            track(_count_logger("n_units", float(len(corpus.units))))
431	            track(_count_logger("n_relations", float(len(corpus.relations))))
432	            # split ids must reference real corpus units (no silent partition drift, audit H4)
433	            if StudyMode.DISCOVERY in modes:
434	                assert plan.split is not None
435	                unit_ids = {u.unit_id for u in corpus.units}
436	                unknown = (set(plan.split.discovery_ids) | set(plan.split.confirm_ids)) - unit_ids
437	                if unknown:
438	                    raise FirewallViolation(
439	                        f"split references {len(unknown)} id(s) absent from the corpus "
440	                        f"(e.g. {sorted(unknown)[:3]}) — partition would silently drop units"
441	                    )
442	            # optional dataset features (recorded + returned; the baseline builder is blind-built
443	            # from the corpus, so features are provenance/output, not a baseline input here)
444	            feature_matrices: list[FeatureMatrix] = []
445	            corpus_unit_ids = {u.unit_id for u in corpus.units}
446	            for fb in defn.feature_builders:
447	                fm = fb.build(corpus)
448	                # features must describe THIS corpus's units — no fictional or duplicate ids
449	                # (the feature-side analogue of the split-drift guard above).
450	                if len(set(fm.unit_ids)) != len(fm.unit_ids):
451	                    raise FirewallViolation(
452	                        f"feature builder {list(fb.provides)} returned duplicate unit_ids"
453	                    )
454	                ghost = set(fm.unit_ids) - corpus_unit_ids
455	                if ghost:
456	                    raise FirewallViolation(
457	                        f"feature builder {list(fb.provides)} returned {len(ghost)} unit_id(s) "
458	                        f"absent from the corpus (e.g. {sorted(ghost)[:3]})"
459	                    )
460	                feature_matrices.append(fm)
461	                trail.record(
462	                    "features",
463	                    f"built feature matrix ({len(fm.feature_names)} features)",
464	                    provides=list(fb.provides),
465	                    n_units=len(fm.unit_ids),
466	                    feature_names=list(fm.feature_names),
467	                )
468	
469	        # ---- BASELINE (blind — Firewall A). Capture the ordering instant BEFORE the build. ----

content.text:
The finding is confirmed by the code. Line 435 builds `unit_ids = {u.unit_id for u in corpus.units}` inside the `if StudyMode.DISCOVERY in modes:` branch, and line 445 builds an identical `corpus_unit_ids = {u.unit_id for u in corpus.units}` unconditionally just before the feature builder loop.

Let me check whether `corpus.units` could be different objects, or whether `corpus_unit_ids` is actually used regardless of feature builders.

input.description: Check usage of the two id sets

content.text:
The finding is accurate. Both lines build the identical set from the same `corpus.units` iterable. In DISCOVERY mode with feature builders, the corpus units are iterated twice to produce the same `{unit_id}` set.

I attempted to refute by checking:
1. Whether the two sets differ — they don't; both are `{u.unit_id for u in corpus.units}` over the same `corpus` object, no mutation between lines 435 and 445.
2. Whether `corpus_unit_ids` is only built when needed — it's built unconditionally at line 445 (outside the feature-builder loop), so even though it's only *used* inside the loop, the set is always constructed.

The only mild nuance: `corpus_unit_ids` at line 445 is built unconditionally even if there are zero feature builders, but the finding's core claim — that DISCOVERY + feature builders causes two redundant O(U) passes building the identical set — is correct. The `unit_ids` from line 435 could simply be reused for the ghost check.

input.reasoning: Confirmed by reading src/assay_engine/pipeline.py lines 433-454. Line 435 builds `unit_ids = {u.unit_id for u in corpus.units}` inside the `if StudyMode.DISCOVERY in modes:` branch (used at line 436 for split-id validation). Line 445 builds an identical `corpus_unit_ids = {u.unit_id for u in corpus.units}` unconditionally, used at line 454 for the feature-builder ghost-id check. Both comprehensions iterate the same unmutated `corpus.units` and produce the same set. When a study is in DISCOVERY mode and declares `defn.feature_builders`, both lines execute, causing two redundant O(U) passes constructing the identical set. No code path between the two lines mutates the corpus, the sets are not semantically distinct, and `corpus_unit_ids` is not lazily guarded — it is built unconditionally. The finding's claim holds; the fix (reuse the line-435 set, or hoist a single set computed once) is trivially correct. Could not disprove.

content.text: The finding survives. Both lines 435 and 445 in `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py` build the identical `{u.unit_id for u in corpus.units}` set from the same unmutated corpus, causing a redundant O(U) pass in the common DISCOVERY-mode-with-feature-builders case.
